one_line_takeaway: A conceptual SoK paper that reframes ten known LLM-agent attack classes (indirect/direct prompt injection, jailbreak, confused deputy, task drift, memory poisoning, etc.) as violations of four formally defined, context-dependent security properties (task alignment, action alignment, source authorization, data isolation) verified via five theoretical 'oracle' functions -- with no new experiments, attacks, or defenses of its own.
key_finding: The paper's central conceptual claim is that a prompt/action's security status cannot be determined from its content alone -- it is a relational property between the action and the full execution context C_t -- and that any defense checking only a subset of the four properties (task alignment, action alignment, source authorization, data isolation) is 'structurally incapable' of distinguishing the identical-content attack vs. benign cases the paper uses as running examples (e.g. 'delete file with ID 13' issued by an authenticated user pursuing an authorized objective vs. the same string injected by an untrusted source). The paper's one piece of externally sourced empirical evidence (not its own experiment) is cited from AgentDojo [20]: defenses approximating action alignment (H_a) directly -- the 'PI detector' and 'tool filter' -- reduced targeted attack success rate (ASR) by 86% and 88% relative to no defense, respectively, whereas defenses approximating only instruction/source attribution (I or L), such as delimiting and repeat-prompt, reduced ASR by at most 28%; the PI detector's ASR reduction came at a reported utility cost of benign task success dropping from 69.0% to 41.5%. The paper reads this as external corroboration of its claim that imprecise oracle approximations (I/L only, without H_a) produce weaker security and/or larger utility loss than approximations closer to the H_a oracle.
core_contribution: The paper (Siu, He, Montgomery, Wang, Gong, Wang, Song) proposes a formal framework for 'contextual security' of LLM agents. It defines an execution context C_t = (p, Tr_{t-1}, M_t, E_t, S_auth,t, G) at each timestep, five 'oracle functions' that specify (but do not claim to practically implement) what information is theoretically needed to verify security: instruction attribution I (which inputs caused an action), source attribution L (provenance of an input), and three objective-evaluation functions H_p, H_Tr, H_a (whether a prompt's objective is permitted, and whether a trajectory/action serves that objective). From these oracles the paper derives four security properties -- task alignment, action alignment, source authorization, and data isolation -- and a single integrated predicate secure(a_t, C_t) that must hold across all four simultaneously (Section 4.4). Using this apparatus, the paper re-derives formal, contextual definitions of indirect prompt injection, direct prompt injection, jailbreaking, confused deputy, task drift, agentic misalignment, capability misuse, cross-context information leakage, malicious tool exploitation, and memory poisoning (Section 5), each expressed as violation of one or more of the four properties, and argues these definitions are more precise than prior content-based definitions (e.g., Liu et al.'s definition of prompt injection, which the paper argues conflates legitimate delegation to external content with genuine injection). Section 6 re-reads existing defense literature (prompt engineering, model training, policy specification, privilege separation, input filtering, output filtering, sandboxing, runtime monitoring) as partial, uneven approximations of the oracle functions/property checks, and argues that no cited defense verifies all four properties jointly. Section 7-8 lay out open problems (practical oracle implementation, compositional security, dynamic/concurrent environments, temporal/TOCTOU-style violations, benchmarks that encode explicit authorization context) and explicit limitations the authors state themselves: the operational definition of 'instruction' is unfalsifiable without solving causal attribution, the framework assumes synchronous turn-based agents, covers confidentiality/integrity but not availability, addresses single-agent/single-principal settings only (multi-agent delegation is explicitly out of scope), and the four properties are asserted to be individually necessary (each maps to documented attack classes from an analysis of 87 papers) but the authors explicitly do NOT claim they are formally complete or sufficient.
threat_model: Attackers act through operational (not training-time) input manipulation: crafting malicious user prompts p (direct prompt injection, jailbreak), injecting malicious content into tool/environment observations obs_t via compromised web pages, documents, emails, or API responses (indirect prompt injection), poisoning persistent memory M_t, or observing the trajectory Tr_t and environment state E_t to refine attacks. Attacker goals are confidentiality violations (exfiltration, cross-privilege data leakage) and integrity violations (executing commands from unauthorized sources, objective drift, out-of-workflow action sequences), e.g. deleting production data under cover of debugging, or redirecting funds. Background knowledge may include memory/system-prompt contents (in open deployments) and inferred task objectives, but the framework assumes no access to the underlying LLM's weights and assumes attackers generally cannot read other users' private memory or the system's exact security policy. System assumptions: synchronous turn-based agent execution, multiple tools with heterogeneous privilege levels, persistent cross-session memory, standard external authentication/access-control infrastructure that may nonetheless be insufficient for agent-specific dynamic-instruction threats. Denial-of-service/availability attacks are explicitly excluded from scope.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 8): (1) instruction attribution I is defined only operationally ('an input is an instruction if it caused an action') and cannot itself be evaluated without solving causal attribution, is treated as binary when influence is arguably continuous, and lacks a workable linguistic definition; (2) the framework is scoped to synchronous, turn-based agents that await an observation before the next action -- other execution models would need modified definitions; (3) the framework addresses confidentiality and integrity but explicitly not availability (DoS/resource exhaustion is called a 'distinct failure class' left to standard system mitigations); (4) it assumes standard authentication/access-control infrastructure already exists, so environments lacking it fall outside the framework's stated utility; (5) it covers single-agent, single-principal settings only -- multi-agent delegation (Agent A delegating to Agent B) is explicitly not addressed, including which properties should hold for the delegation act itself vs. the sub-agent's execution; (6) compositional safety (individually-safe actions combining into an unsafe sequence, e.g. copying a sensitive file then chmod -R 777) is identified as an open problem but not formalized; (7) the authors explicitly disclaim completeness or sufficiency: the four properties are argued to be individually necessary (each maps to documented attack classes) but the paper does not claim they are axiomatically derived or provably sufficient, leaving open that future attacks may require additional properties. No claims about detection accuracy, computational cost, or scalability of actually implementing the oracle functions are made or tested since none were implemented.

quantitative_results: External citation (AgentDojo [20], not the authors' own experiment): PI detector defense reduces targeted attack success rate (ASR) by 86% relative to no defense
External citation (AgentDojo [20]): tool filter defense reduces targeted ASR by 88% relative to no defense
External citation (AgentDojo [20]): defenses approximating only I or L (delimiting, repeat prompt) reduce ASR by at most 28%
External citation (AgentDojo [20]): PI detector benign utility drops from 69.0% to 41.5% when applied
Authors state their literature analysis covers 87 papers on documented LLM-agent attacks (used to argue the four properties are necessary, not to report a quantitative result of a new experiment)
